options.feedback_correct: Correct. 1-2mg/kg IV over 5 minutes. Response within 30-60 minutes — MetHb should fall, cyanosis should resolve, SpO2 should normalise. Warn him his urine will turn blue. Tell him before he finds out himself.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "Tell him about the urine before he uses the bathroom. If you don't, you will have an entirely new problem on your hands."
options.pt_remark_correct: My urine is going to be what colour. Right. Fine. How long does this actually take.

options.text: Methylene blue 10mg/kg IV — higher dose for faster response
options.feedback_wrong: At doses above 7mg/kg, methylene blue paradoxically causes methemoglobinemia — it overwhelms the NADPH pathway and behaves as an oxidant rather than a reducing agent. The therapeutic window is narrow. More is actively harmful here.
options.pt_remark_wrong: More of it works faster. Sure. That's always how medicine works.

options.text: Oral methylene blue tablets
options.feedback_wrong: Oral methylene blue has variable and unreliable absorption and is not appropriate for acute symptomatic methemoglobinemia at this severity. IV administration is required for a reliable and rapid effect.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A tablet. For something that's in my blood. You want me to swallow a tablet.

options.text: Continue oxygen and observe — MetHb may self-correct
options.feedback_wrong: At MetHb 38% with tachycardia and ongoing absorption, watchful waiting is not appropriate. The endogenous NADH-dependent reductase pathway is slow. His level is high enough that active treatment is indicated.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Just watch it. You said the level was bad and you want to watch it.

management.title: Address the source
management.hint: Mikkel has grey lips after two years. The master's hands have been blue for fifteen. The vats are still running.

options.text: Notify occupational health authorities, remove him from exposure, arrange coworker testing
options.feedback_correct: Correct and mandatory. This is an occupational poisoning event affecting multiple known workers. Reporting is not optional. He cannot return to the vats without adequate protection. Mikkel needs a MetHb level. The master needs a MetHb level.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "The master has been blue-handed for fifteen years and nobody has ever measured his methaemoglobin. Think about what that number looks like. Think about how long it has looked like that."
options.pt_remark_correct: You're reporting the works. Does my master need to know my specific level, or just that I was treated. Because there's a difference.

options.text: Advise him to wear gloves going forward
options.feedback_wrong: Individual advice without reporting leaves every other worker unprotected. Mikkel has two years of exposure. The master has fifteen. Telling one sixteen-year-old to wear gloves and sending him back is not adequate medicine.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Gloves. That's it. So I just go back tomorrow and wear gloves. Great.

options.text: Write a letter to his master explaining the diagnosis
options.feedback_wrong: A letter is not a report. It cannot compel any change and leaves workers currently at the vats exposed. This requires occupational health authorities — not a note handed to a teenager to carry back to the man who sent him here.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A letter for me to take back. To my master. Who sent me here.

options.text: Nothing further — the patient is your only concern
options.feedback_wrong: An identifiable occupational hazard causing acute poisoning in multiple known workers carries a mandatory reporting obligation. Treating one patient and returning him to the source is not medicine.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Fine by me honestly. Can I just go then.

management.title: Disposition
management.hint: MetHb was 38%. Methylene blue given. Sixteen years old. Still being monitored. Where does he go?

options.text: Admit for serial MetHb levels, cardiac monitoring, repeat methylene blue if needed
options.feedback_correct: Correct. MetHb 38% with tachycardia warrants admission. Serial co-oximetry to confirm falling levels — rebound can occur. Cardiac monitoring. Methylene blue may need repeating. He is sixteen and was still absorbing dye on arrival.
options.pt_remark_correct: How long. Someone needs to tell my family. Not my master. My family. There's a difference.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "Serial co-oximetry to confirm falling levels. Rebound can occur as aniline continues to absorb from skin. Cardiac monitoring for the myocardial injury. And someone needs to call the hall."

options.text: Discharge once MetHb falls below 20% post-treatment
options.feedback_wrong: A single improving MetHb level is not sufficient for discharge. Aniline has ongoing dermal absorption potential and rebound methemoglobinemia can occur. Serial levels and cardiac monitoring are required before discharge is safe.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Once I'm feeling better I can go. How long is that going to take.
options.fennick_endgame_comment: "MetHb at 20 percent is still symptomatic. Aniline has ongoing dermal absorption. Rebound methemoglobinemia occurs. He would have deteriorated at home without anyone watching."
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "20% is still symptomatic. Treat to resolution, not to an arbitrary threshold."

options.text: Discharge with oral antidote and outpatient follow-up in one week
options.feedback_wrong: There is no oral antidote regimen for acute methemoglobinemia at this severity. MetHb 38% with tachycardia in a sixteen-year-old is not an outpatient situation.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Come back in a week. For the thing that turned my blood brown.
options.fennick_endgame_comment: "An oral antidote that does not exist, for a level that required IV treatment. He would have gone home still poisoned."
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Methylene blue is IV only. There is no oral formulation."

options.text: Transfer to burns unit for chemical decontamination
options.feedback_wrong: This is a systemic toxidrome, not primarily a skin injury. The burns unit manages cutaneous chemical burns — the appropriate setting here is a monitored medical bed with access to co-oximetry and repeat methylene blue.
options.pt_remark_wrong: The burns ward. Because of some blue on my arms.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "This is haematological toxicity, not a chemical burn. Wrong specialty."

report_template.presenting: three days of progressive central cyanosis following occupational aniline dye exposure at the Ashgate Textile Works

pertinent_positives.addl-hx: eight to ten hours daily bare-handed aniline vat dyeing, eating at the dye bench, symptoms worsening end of shift, coworker with grey lips after two years, master with blue hands for fifteen years
pertinent_positives.pe: central and peripheral cyanosis with grey-blue lips and slate fingernail beds, normal respiratory effort, clear lung fields, SpO2 85% on pulse oximetry with no respiratory distress
pertinent_positives.labs: normal pO2 98 on ABG directly contradicting pulse oximeter reading — paradox confirms dyshemoglobinaemia, compensatory tachycardia HR 108
pertinent_positives.imaging: CXR without acute findings — lungs are not the source
pertinent_positives.special: co-oximetry MetHb 38% with chocolate-brown blood on draw, confirming methemoglobinemia from aniline dye dermal absorption

report_template.assessment: Methemoglobinemia — occupational, aniline dye dermal absorption, MetHb 38%
report_template.plan: skin decontamination, G6PD screen, methylene blue 1-2mg/kg IV, serial co-oximetry, admit for monitoring, occupational health reporting, coworker testing

explanation: Methemoglobinemia occurs when haemoglobin iron is oxidised from ferrous (Fe2+) to ferric (Fe3+), forming MetHb which cannot carry oxygen. Classic causes include aniline dyes, dapsone, nitrites, benzocaine. Key diagnostic finding: cyanosis with normal pO2 on ABG — pulse oximetry reads approximately 85% regardless of actual MetHb. Blood appears chocolate-brown. Antidote: methylene blue 1-2mg/kg IV — requires G6PD (check first). G6PD deficient: ascorbic acid. High-dose methylene blue (>7mg/kg) paradoxically worsens MetHb.

explanation_detail.who_gets_it: Workers with aniline/nitrite/dapsone exposure. Infants vulnerable to dietary nitrates. Drug-induced: benzocaine, prilocaine, dapsone, metoclopramide in high doses. G6PD deficient patients at higher risk.
explanation_detail.risk_factors: Occupational aniline or nitrite exposure, lack of protective equipment, ingestion of nitrates, drug-induced causes.
explanation_detail.typical_presentation: Central cyanosis unresponsive to oxygen. SpO2 fixed around 85% regardless of severity — the co-oximeter artefact. Normal pO2 on ABG. Chocolate-brown blood on draw. Headache, fatigue, tachycardia. Severe: confusion, seizures, dysrhythmia.
explanation_detail.moa: Fe3+ MetHb cannot bind O2 — also causes left shift of O2 dissociation curve in remaining functional haemoglobin. NADH-MetHb reductase is the primary endogenous reduction pathway. Methylene blue activates the NADPH pathway via G6PD — rapidly reduces Fe3+ back to Fe2+.
explanation_detail.step1_buzzwords: Cyanosis + normal pO2, SpO2 ~85% artefact, chocolate-brown blood, aniline/nitrate/dapsone, methylene blue antidote, G6PD check first, high-dose methylene blue worsens.
explanation_detail.lab_buzzwords: ABG: normal pO2 with low pulse ox SpO2 = dyshemoglobinaemia. Co-oximetry: direct MetHb measurement. Standard pulse ox unreliable. Hgb normal — not anaemia.
explanation_detail.treatment: Methylene blue 1-2mg/kg IV over 5 min if G6PD normal. Repeat at 1mg/kg if no response at 1 hour. Maximum ~7mg/kg total. G6PD deficient: ascorbic acid 1-2g IV. Exchange transfusion for refractory cases. Remove from exposure. Skin decontamination.

fennick_opening: "His master walked him to the door and left."
cc_after_addl_hx: Everyone at the vats looks like this. My master's hands have been blue for fifteen years and he's fine. Nobody said anything to him.
cc_after_pe: You're looking at my nails like that. They've been that colour for two weeks. I thought it was just the dye getting in.
cc_after_imaging: The lungs are fine. So it's not the lungs. Then what is it.
cc_after_hint: The dye. You think it's the dye. I knew it was the dye. I've known for a week.
cc_after_dx: Right. It's in my blood. Not just on my hands — it's actually in my blood.

title: The Politician's Figure

chart.cc: My tailor flagged it first, which I found embarrassing. I've gained considerable weight over the past year, centralised I'm told. My face has changed. I assumed stress. I have a significant schedule so I'd appreciate efficiency.
chart.hpi: The Politician arrived with his aide, who was asked to wait outside and was not used to being asked to wait outside. He has gained fourteen pounds over eighteen months — weight he describes as redistributing rather than generalising. His face is rounder than his Assembly portrait. He has noted easy bruising, attributed to a campaign event. His blood pressure has been elevated at three consecutive private consultations. He reports muscle fatigue on stairs. He has had intermittent irritability attributed to the political climate. He asks three times whether this consultation is confidential. On direct questioning he reports taking an oral corticosteroid prescribed privately eighteen months ago for lumbar radiculopathy — he has continued it without review because it controls the pain.
chart.pmh: Hypertension — diagnosed age 37. Chronic low back pain.
chart.sh: Member of the Ferrovane Assembly, southern district. Married. Lives in the Assembly quarter. Does not smoke. Drinks at official functions — 'socially, not habitually.'
chart.meds_on_addl_hx: Prednisone 20mg daily — ongoing, prescribed privately for lumbar radiculopathy.

additional_hx: He answers in full sentences with the cadence of a man accustomed to being quoted. Has not brought a medication list because he considers that the physician's job to obtain. When you ask directly about all medications including things he might not consider medications — he pauses. Then mentions the prednisone. 20mg daily, eighteen months, private physician keeps renewing, it works for the back so he has not questioned it. He had not considered it relevant to weight and face changes. When you explain it is in fact the most relevant thing he has said, he looks briefly as though he is recalculating something. He asks three times whether this consultation is confidential. His aide is still outside.
patient_quote_addl_hx: "The prednisone is for my back. It has nothing to do with my face. My private physician has reviewed my bloods regularly and not flagged anything. I'm not sure why a walk-in clinic would have a different view."
pe_findings: Moon facies — rounded, plethoric. Supraclavicular and dorsocervical fat pads. Central adiposity with relatively thin extremities. Skin: thin, translucent. Multiple easy bruises on forearms. Wide purple striae on abdomen and flanks — patient unaware of them. Proximal muscle bulk reduced bilaterally. BP 158/96. No peripheral oedema. No signs of cardiac failure.
imaging: CXR: Normal. No adrenal mass visible — CT is the appropriate modality. Not indicated on plain film.

special_test.name: Late-Night Salivary Cortisol x2 + Overnight Dexamethasone Suppression Test
special_test.result: Late-night salivary cortisol: 14.2 nmol/L and 13.8 nmol/L on two collections. Morning cortisol post-dexamethasone: 480 nmol/L. Plasma ACTH: <1 pg/mL.

tests.name: CBC
tests.pt_remark: I had a full panel done privately three months ago. I'll have my aide send the results.

tests.name: CMP
tests.result: Glucose 128 (HIGH — glucocorticoid-induced insulin resistance, impaired glucose tolerance). K 3.1 (LOW — cortisol has mild mineralocorticoid activity). Creatinine 1.0 (normal). LFTs normal. Na 144.
tests.pt_remark: I had a working breakfast. Eggs. Coffee. I assume that's within acceptable parameters.

tests.name: EKG
tests.result: Normal sinus rhythm. LVH voltage criteria. No ischemic changes.
tests.pt_remark: I've had these annually for six years. Nothing has ever been flagged.

tests.name: Troponin
tests.result: Negative. No myocardial injury.

tests.name: Lipase
tests.result: Normal. Not relevant.

tests.name: UA
tests.result: Glucose 2+. No protein. No blood. No infection.

tests.name: ABG/VBG
tests.result: Normal. pH 7.42, pO2 96, pCO2 39. No respiratory compromise.

tests.name: Coags
tests.result: Normal. PT/INR 1.0. PTT 28. Note: chronic glucocorticoid excess does increase thrombotic risk — DVT prophylaxis worth considering.

hints: Central obesity, moon facies, buffalo hump, easy bruising, wide purple striae, proximal weakness, hypertension, hyperglycaemia. These are not separate problems accumulated over forty years. They are a single unifying syndrome with a name.

Before you work up endogenous Cushing's — pituitary MRI, adrenal CT, CRH stimulation — you must ask about exogenous steroids. He did not volunteer this information. He considers it his private business. You have to ask directly and specifically.

The ACTH is suppressed. Suppressed ACTH with elevated cortisol means the cortisol is not being driven from above — not pituitary, not ectopic. Either the adrenal is producing autonomously, or the cortisol is coming from outside the body entirely. What has he been taking for eighteen months?

Exogenous steroid therapy suppresses the HPA axis. You cannot stop prednisone abruptly — eighteen months of 20mg daily means his adrenal cortex has been idle for over a year. Abrupt cessation causes acute adrenal insufficiency. The taper must be slow. Weeks to months.

management.title: Confirm the diagnosis biochemically
management.hint: You suspect Cushing syndrome clinically. What do you order to confirm cortisol excess before any imaging or invasive workup?

options.text: Late-night salivary cortisol x2 or 24hr urine free cortisol
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Cortisol normally nadirs at midnight. Elevated late-night salivary cortisol on two separate collections confirms loss of diurnal rhythm. 24hr urine free cortisol is an alternative. Either establishes hypercortisolism biochemically before imaging.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "Late-night cortisol because cortisol nadirs at midnight. A random daytime level proves nothing — stress alone raises it. You need the trough."
options.pt_remark_correct: You want me to spit into a tube at midnight, twice, at home? I have a sitting at nine the morning after both times, you understand that.

options.text: Random serum cortisol
options.feedback_wrong: Random cortisol is unreliable — cortisol fluctuates enormously throughout the day and with acute stress. A single elevated result proves nothing. A single normal result excludes nothing. Timed or 24hr collections are required.
options.pt_remark_wrong: One blood test and that's this part done? That seems efficient, let's do that.

options.text: CT abdomen/pelvis to look for adrenal mass
options.feedback_wrong: Do not image before you have confirmed biochemical hypercortisolism. Adrenal incidentalomas are common — finding a small adrenal mass without biochemical confirmation leads to unnecessary workup and surgery. Establish the hormone excess first.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A scan — quick, definitive, no tubes at midnight. I'd prefer that.

options.text: MRI pituitary with contrast
options.feedback_wrong: Pituitary MRI is appropriate for ACTH-dependent Cushing's — but you have not yet confirmed hypercortisolism, and you have not yet measured ACTH. Imaging the pituitary now is several steps ahead of where you are. Establish biochemistry first.
options.pt_remark_wrong: You want to look at my brain? Is there something else you haven't told me.

management.title: Determine the source — ACTH
management.hint: Hypercortisolism confirmed. ACTH level is the single most important next step. What does a suppressed ACTH tell you?

options.text: Plasma ACTH level
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Low or suppressed ACTH means the cortisol is not being driven by the pituitary or ectopic source — adrenal autonomous production or exogenous source. High ACTH means pituitary or ectopic. This single result dictates the entire subsequent workup.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "Low ACTH means the signal from the pituitary has stopped. The cortisol is coming from somewhere else — adrenal autonomous production, or something he is putting in his body. Both are possible until you ask the right question."
options.pt_remark_correct: More blood, fine. What's this one actually measuring.

options.text: CRH stimulation test
options.feedback_wrong: CRH stimulation is used to differentiate pituitary from ectopic ACTH-dependent Cushing's — after ACTH has already been found to be elevated. You have not measured ACTH yet. You are skipping a step.
options.pt_remark_wrong: That takes several hours apparently. I have a delegation at two o'clock.

options.text: Inferior petrosal sinus sampling
options.feedback_wrong: Inferior petrosal sinus sampling is an invasive procedure used to confirm pituitary source when ACTH-dependent Cushing's has already been established and non-invasive tests are inconclusive. You are several investigations away from this being relevant.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A catheter into my brain. For a diagnosis you haven't made yet.

options.text: Proceed to adrenal CT — suppressed ACTH assumed
options.feedback_wrong: Do not assume. Measure. An elevated ACTH would completely redirect the workup away from the adrenal gland. This test takes hours and changes everything. Do it first.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Skip the blood test, go straight to the scan. That's what I'd vote for, for what it's worth.

management.title: Connect the ACTH result to the source
management.hint: ACTH suppressed. Cortisol elevated. You know he has been on prednisone for eighteen months. What does this tell you about the source of his cortisol excess?

options.text: Exogenous prednisone explains the suppressed ACTH — no further imaging needed
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Suppressed ACTH plus eighteen months of exogenous prednisone is the complete picture. The adrenal gland is healthy but dormant. No CT, no MRI, no IPSS. The diagnosis is in the medication history.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "Suppressed ACTH. Eighteen months of exogenous prednisone. The adrenal is healthy and dormant. The diagnosis is in the medication history. No CT required."
options.pt_remark_correct: So the prednisone did all of this. The one that was renewed every three months without anyone asking questions.

options.text: Order adrenal CT — ACTH-independent confirmed
options.sub: Adrenal CT without connecting medication history
options.feedback_wrong: You have the answer already. Suppressed ACTH plus eighteen months of exogenous prednisone is the complete picture. Imaging the adrenal adds nothing and delays the correct management.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A scan. Good. That's the kind of answer I came here for.

options.text: Refer to endocrinology for full Cushing's workup
options.feedback_wrong: You have enough to make the diagnosis. Suppressed ACTH, elevated cortisol, eighteen months of exogenous prednisone. Refer after confirming the source — not before.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A specialist, fine. Someone who can see me this week I assume.

options.text: Genetic testing for familial Cushing syndrome
options.feedback_wrong: Familial Cushing syndrome is rare and not your first thought in a forty-year-old with a clear exogenous steroid history. The diagnosis is already in front of you.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Genetic. So this was always going to happen to me.

management.title: Managing exogenous Cushing syndrome — the steroid
management.hint: Iatrogenic Cushing syndrome from 18 months of prednisone 20mg. His HPA axis is suppressed. What do you do with the prednisone?

options.text: Gradual prednisone taper over weeks to months with endocrinology guidance
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Eighteen months of supraphysiological glucocorticoid suppresses the adrenal cortex. Abrupt cessation causes acute adrenal insufficiency — hypotension, vomiting, collapse. The taper must be slow enough to allow HPA axis recovery — weeks to months. Endocrinology must be involved.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "The axis needs time. Weeks to months. The speed of the taper depends on how long it was suppressed and how the patient tolerates each step."
options.pt_remark_correct: Months to stop a tablet. So I keep taking it while stopping it? That's going to be difficult to explain to my schedule.

options.text: Stop prednisone immediately — the source of harm must be removed
options.feedback_wrong: Abrupt cessation of long-term high-dose steroids causes acute adrenal insufficiency — a life-threatening emergency. His HPA axis has been suppressed for eighteen months. The adrenal cortex cannot produce cortisol on demand. Never stop abruptly.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Stop it today — remove the problem. That's reasonable isn't it.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Acute adrenal insufficiency within 24 hours. His axis has been idle for eighteen months."
options.fennick_endgame_comment: "His adrenal cortex had been idle for eighteen months. You stopped the tablet and asked it to produce cortisol by morning. It could not. He would have collapsed within 24 hours."

options.text: Continue prednisone and add ketoconazole to block cortisol synthesis
options.feedback_wrong: Ketoconazole is used to block endogenous cortisol synthesis in Cushing's Disease or ectopic ACTH syndrome. Here the cortisol is exogenous — from the prednisone itself. Blocking adrenal synthesis while continuing to give oral prednisone makes no sense.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Two medications to fix the problem caused by one medication. I'm not sure that's the right direction.

options.text: Bilateral adrenalectomy
options.feedback_wrong: Bilateral adrenalectomy is a last resort for refractory ACTH-dependent Cushing's. His adrenal glands are normal. The problem is exogenous. Surgery on healthy glands is not indicated.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Remove both adrenal glands for a back pain medication I've been taking for eighteen months. I'm going to need a second opinion.

management.title: Disposition and complications
management.hint: Glucose 128, BP 158/96, hypokalemia, LVH. He is going home. What goes in the discharge plan?

options.text: Discharge — prednisone taper plan, endocrinology referral, optimise antihypertensives, glucose monitoring, potassium supplementation
options.feedback_correct: Correct. He is clinically stable. He needs a formal taper plan, endocrinology follow-up, blood pressure optimisation, monitoring for steroid-induced diabetes, and potassium supplementation for the hypokalaemia. Also counsel on bone protection given 18 months of steroid exposure.
options.pt_remark_correct: Discharge with a plan, fine. I need written documentation — not for legal reasons, I just need to know what to tell my private physician. Who should have flagged this.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "Stable. Outpatient. The complications — glucose, potassium, blood pressure, bone — all need follow-up. But none of them need a hospital bed."

options.text: Admit for immediate supervised steroid cessation
options.feedback_wrong: He is clinically stable and does not need inpatient admission. The taper is a slow outpatient process over weeks. Admission is not indicated and would not change the management timeline.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Admitted? I have Assembly sessions. Who do I speak to about a private room.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Abrupt cessation after eighteen months of steroids. Adrenal crisis within hours."

options.text: Discharge with no follow-up — now that source is identified, the body will self-correct
options.feedback_wrong: HPA axis recovery requires monitoring. Glucose 128 requires follow-up. K 3.1 requires supplementation. BP 158/96 requires optimisation. LVH requires cardiovascular follow-up. Discharging without a plan is not appropriate.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Come back in a week and it sorts itself out? My tailor spent forty minutes remeasuring me last month.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "You identified the source. You did not confirm the axis will recover."

options.text: Refer to bariatric surgery for central obesity
options.feedback_wrong: The central obesity, moon facies, and fat redistribution are directly caused by glucocorticoid excess. They will resolve as the steroid is tapered. Referring for bariatric surgery before addressing the root cause is profoundly wrong.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Weight loss surgery. For weight gained from a drug you're now going to stop. I genuinely cannot tell if this is serious.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "The central obesity is a consequence of hypercortisolaemia. Treat the cause."

fennick_case_complete: "The prednisone has been on his shelf for eighteen months. He may not have known what it was doing to him."
"He'll ask about his cognition before he leaves. Answer it honestly."

report_template.pmh: hypertension and chronic low back pain
report_template.presenting: eighteen months of progressive central weight gain, moon facies, easy bruising, hypertension, and proximal muscle fatigue

pertinent_positives.addl-hx: prednisone 20mg daily for 18 months prescribed by private physician for lumbar radiculopathy, not volunteered until directly asked, no medication review in 18 months
pertinent_positives.pe: moon facies, dorsocervical and supraclavicular fat pads, central adiposity, wide purple abdominal striae, easy bruising, proximal muscle bulk reduced, BP 158/96
pertinent_positives.labs: glucose 128 impaired fasting, hypokalaemia K 3.1, leukocytosis with neutrophilia, glycosuria on UA
pertinent_positives.imaging: CXR with LVH pattern only, no acute findings
pertinent_positives.special: late-night salivary cortisol elevated on two collections, dexamethasone suppression test failed to suppress, ACTH suppressed confirming ACTH-independent exogenous Cushing syndrome

report_template.assessment: Cushing Syndrome — iatrogenic, exogenous glucocorticoid excess from 18 months of prednisone 20mg daily
report_template.plan: gradual prednisone taper with endocrinology guidance, optimise antihypertensives, potassium supplementation, glucose monitoring, bone protection, follow-up cortisol and HPA axis recovery testing

explanation: Cushing Syndrome is the clinical state of chronic glucocorticoid excess from any source. The most common cause worldwide is exogenous steroid therapy. Cushing Disease specifically refers to pituitary ACTH-secreting adenoma. Key: confirm hypercortisolism biochemically first (late-night salivary cortisol, 24hr UFC, dexamethasone suppression), then measure ACTH to determine source. Suppressed ACTH = ACTH-independent (exogenous or adrenal). Never stop long-term steroids abruptly — taper to allow HPA axis recovery.

explanation_detail.who_gets_it: Exogenous: anyone on long-term glucocorticoids — commonest cause. Endogenous: pituitary adenoma (Cushing Disease, 70%), adrenal adenoma (15%), ectopic ACTH (15% — SCLC, carcinoid).
explanation_detail.risk_factors: Long-term glucocorticoid use, pituitary adenoma, adrenal tumour, SCLC or carcinoid for ectopic ACTH.
explanation_detail.typical_presentation: Central obesity, moon facies, buffalo hump, supraclavicular fat pads. Skin: wide purple striae, easy bruising, thin skin, poor healing. Proximal myopathy. Hypertension, hyperglycaemia, hypokalaemia. Osteoporosis. Mood disturbance, cognitive effects. Immunosuppression.
explanation_detail.moa: Glucocorticoid excess: promotes gluconeogenesis and fat redistribution to central depots. Impairs insulin signalling. Promotes catabolism of skin and muscle. Mineralocorticoid activity causes potassium wasting. HPA axis suppression from exogenous steroids — adrenal cortex becomes atrophic from disuse.
explanation_detail.step1_buzzwords: Central obesity, moon facies, buffalo hump, purple striae, easy bruising, proximal myopathy, hypertension, hyperglycaemia, hypokalaemia. Suppressed ACTH = ACTH-independent. Never stop abruptly.
explanation_detail.lab_buzzwords: Late-night salivary cortisol (x2) or 24hr UFC. Overnight dexamethasone suppression — normal <50 nmol/L. ACTH: low = ACTH-independent (exogenous/adrenal); high = ACTH-dependent (pituitary/ectopic). Leukocytosis, hyperglycaemia, hypokalaemia.
explanation_detail.treatment: Exogenous: gradual taper to allow HPA recovery — weeks to months. Endocrine follow-up. Manage complications. Pituitary disease: transsphenoidal surgery. Adrenal adenoma: unilateral adrenalectomy. Ectopic ACTH: treat primary source. Never abrupt cessation of long-term steroids.

cc_after_addl_hx: The prednisone is for my back. I have been taking it because it works. I don't see how a back medication is responsible for my face changing.
cc_after_pe: You're looking at my abdomen. Those marks — I thought those were from weight gain. How long have those been there.
cc_after_imaging: The lungs are clear. Good. So it isn't something serious. Can we wrap up.
cc_after_hint: The prednisone. You keep coming back to the prednisone. Fine. What does the prednisone do.
cc_after_dx: Iatrogenic. Meaning it was caused by treatment. Meaning it was preventable. I'd like a moment before we continue.
generic_lab_remark: I had a full panel done privately three months ago. I'll have it sent over.

title: The Potter's Hands

chart.cc: My hands don't want to do what I ask them anymore.
chart.hpi: The Potter arrived with clay dust still in the seams of his coat and dried slip on his cuffs. He has had gradually worsening pain in both hands for just over a year — worst at the base of the thumbs and the finger joints he uses most at the wheel. The pain is worst after long sessions and eases with rest. He says the pain makes him slower, but what troubles him more is that the bowls he throws are no longer as clean and balanced as they were. His wife has noticed he complains more in the mornings. He has had mild morning stiffness — no more than twenty minutes — that loosens once he starts moving. He denies fevers, rash, eye symptoms, or weight loss. He has been working at the wheel for forty years.
chart.meds: Occasional ibuprofen for the pain. No regular medications.
chart.fh: Mother had stiff hands in her sixties. No formal diagnosis recorded.
chart.sh: Studio potter, Ashgate quarter. Lives with his wife. Non-smoker. An occasional drink.

memory_fragments.pe: He has carried a small wooden rib tool in his coat pocket every day for thirty years. It fits the curl of his right palm exactly — worn smooth where his thumb rests. He has started rubbing it between his fingers in waiting rooms. He does not know when he started doing this.
memory_fragments.imaging: The first bowl he ever threw — lopsided, thicker on one side — sits on the windowsill of his studio. He kept it because his teacher told him imperfection is where the piece remembers it was made by a person. He has been thinking about that a lot lately.

additional_hx: He answers carefully, like a man who measures things. Pain pattern: worse after work, better with rest — he is specific about this. Morning stiffness gone within twenty minutes of starting to move. The joints that hurt most are the base of both thumbs and the end joints of his index and middle fingers — the ones that press and shape. No other joint involvement. No swelling he can see — just a bony feeling, a thickening. He tried ibuprofen and it helps somewhat. He pulls the wooden rib from his coat pocket while talking and turns it over in his fingers without noticing.
patient_quote_addl_hx: "The wheel still knows what to do. It's just the hands arguing with me now. Forty years of the same motion — I suppose I should have expected them to have an opinion eventually."
pe_findings: Both hands clay-dusted, well-muscled from decades of work. Bony enlargement of DIP joints bilaterally — Heberden's nodes at index and middle fingers. Tender first CMC joints bilaterally, worse on right. Mild crepitus with thumb opposition and wrist movement. No warm boggy synovitis. No ulnar deviation. No rheumatoid nodules. Grip strength limited by pain rather than weakness. No rash, no eye findings, no lymphadenopathy.
imaging: Hand X-rays bilateral: joint space narrowing at first CMC joints and DIP joints bilaterally. Osteophyte formation. Subchondral sclerosis. No erosive changes. No periarticular osteopenia. No periosteal reaction. Pattern is consistent with degenerative change — not inflammatory arthritis.

special_test.name: Anti-CCP Antibody + Rheumatoid Factor
special_test.result: RF negative. Anti-CCP negative. ESR 22 mm/hr. CRP 4.1 mg/L.

tests.name: CBC
tests.result: Normal. WBC 7.1, Hgb 14.6, Plt 288. No anaemia, no leukocytosis. No systemic inflammatory pattern.
tests.pt_remark: I don't know what you're looking for in that one. But I trust the process.

tests.name: CMP
tests.result: Normal. Creatinine 1.0, glucose 94, LFTs normal, uric acid 5.8 (normal — gout less likely). No metabolic derangement.
tests.pt_remark: I ate this morning. Bread and some cold meat. I work with my hands all day so I eat well.

tests.name: EKG
tests.result: Normal sinus rhythm. Not relevant to this presentation.
tests.pt_remark: My father had something with his heart in his sixties. I've always wondered if I should worry about that.

tests.name: Troponin
tests.result: Negative. Not relevant.

tests.name: Lipase
tests.result: Normal. Not relevant.

tests.name: UA
tests.result: Normal. No haematuria, no crystals, no infection.

tests.name: ABG/VBG
tests.result: Normal. Not indicated here.

tests.name: Coags
tests.result: Normal. Relevant if considering NSAID therapy in context of bleeding risk.

hints: The pain is worse after use and better with rest. In rheumatoid arthritis, morning stiffness lasts over an hour and improves with movement. His stiffness is gone within twenty minutes. That pattern matters.

Look at which joints are involved. DIP joints and first CMC. Rheumatoid arthritis classically spares the DIP joints and attacks the MCP and PIP joints symmetrically. The distribution is telling you something.
The X-ray shows osteophytes and joint space narrowing — no erosions, no periarticular osteopenia. That is the radiological signature of mechanical wear, not autoimmune destruction.

RF negative, anti-CCP negative, ESR only mildly elevated at 22 in a 61-year-old. Inflammatory markers are not supporting synovitis. The mildly elevated ESR is age-appropriate and nonspecific. Don't let a borderline number override a clear pattern.

management.title: Workup direction — where do you start?
management.hint: Bilateral hand pain in a 61-year-old. Before ordering anything — what does the joint distribution and pain character tell you about where to look first?

options.text: Examine joint distribution carefully — order targeted inflammatory markers and hand X-rays
options.feedback_correct: Correct. DIP involvement, pain worse with use, stiffness under 30 minutes — this pattern should direct you toward degenerative rather than inflammatory arthritis before you order anything. Targeted workup rather than a broad inflammatory panel.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "Distribution before diagnosis. DIP involvement, pain worse with use, stiffness under thirty minutes. That pattern is already narrowing your differential before the first test result."
options.pt_remark_correct: You're asking about which joints specifically. Nobody's asked me that before — they just look at the hands as a whole.

options.text: Order full inflammatory panel immediately — RF, anti-CCP, ESR, CRP, ANA
options.feedback_wrong: Not wrong to order inflammatory markers, but ordering a broad panel without first characterising the joint pattern is backward. The distribution and pain character should guide your hypothesis before the labs do.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A full panel. Right. Whatever gives you the most information I suppose.

options.text: Refer directly to rheumatology without further workup
options.feedback_wrong: Rheumatology referral may ultimately be appropriate but referring before any workup means you're sending a patient without a working hypothesis. Basic pattern recognition and initial labs belong in primary care before specialist input.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A specialist. How long would that take. My hands are the problem now, not in six months.

options.text: Start empirical NSAIDs and review in three months
options.feedback_wrong: NSAIDs may be part of the eventual management but starting them without establishing the diagnosis delays recognition of a potentially progressive inflammatory arthritis if that's what this is. Diagnose first.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Anti-inflammatories and come back later. What if it keeps getting worse while we wait.

management.title: Interpreting the inflammatory markers
management.hint: ESR 22. CRP 4.1. Borderline elevated, not clearly abnormal. RF negative. Anti-CCP negative. How do you read this in context?

options.text: Mildly elevated ESR is nonspecific at 61 — does not override the clinical and X-ray pattern
options.feedback_correct: Correct. ESR rises with age — a level of 22 in a 61-year-old is not meaningful evidence of active synovitis. Paired with negative RF, negative anti-CCP, and a radiological picture without erosions, this does not support inflammatory arthritis.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "ESR 22 at 61. That is age. That is the city. It is not synovitis until the rest of the picture says it is."
options.pt_remark_correct: So the number being slightly up doesn't mean what I thought it meant. It just means I'm 61.

options.text: Borderline CRP suggests early inflammatory arthritis — start hydroxychloroquine empirically
options.feedback_wrong: Hydroxychloroquine is a DMARD used in confirmed inflammatory arthritis — RA, lupus, connective tissue disease. Starting it empirically on a borderline CRP in a patient whose clinical and radiological picture points to OA is premature and exposes the patient to unnecessary medication.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A medication for autoimmune disease. Before you're certain that's what this is.

options.text: Negative RF means this cannot be RA — diagnosis is confirmed as OA
options.feedback_wrong: Seronegative RA exists — up to 20% of RA cases are RF negative. Negative serology is helpful evidence but not definitive exclusion. The full picture — joint distribution, pain character, X-ray findings, inflammatory markers — decides the diagnosis together.
options.pt_remark_wrong: The test came back negative so that's settled. I'm glad it's that simple.

options.text: Repeat full inflammatory panel in six weeks to catch early RA
options.feedback_wrong: Repeating the same tests while the clinical and X-ray picture already points clearly to OA does not serve the patient. If this were truly early inflammatory arthritis presenting atypically, the joint distribution and X-ray pattern would not fit — and you have both of those already.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Come back in six weeks. My next commission is in three.

management.title: Confirm the diagnosis — reading the X-ray
management.hint: Osteophytes. Joint space narrowing. No erosions. No periarticular osteopenia. DIP and first CMC involvement. What are you looking at?

options.text: Osteoarthritis — mechanical degenerative pattern, no inflammatory erosive disease
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Osteophytes and joint space narrowing at DIP and CMC joints with no erosions is the radiological hallmark of OA. The absence of periarticular osteopenia rules against inflammatory arthritis. The distribution confirms it.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "Osteophytes at the margins. Narrowing at the surface. No erosions eating through the bone. The joint has been worn down, not attacked."
options.pt_remark_correct: So the X-ray shows wear. Not attack. Just — forty years of asking the same joints to do the same thing.

options.text: Seronegative RA — erosions may not yet be visible on plain film
options.feedback_wrong: Early RA before erosions develop is possible, but the DIP joint involvement, absence of periarticular osteopenia, and osteophyte formation all argue against it. Seronegative RA is a diagnosis of exclusion — not a default when serology is negative.
options.pt_remark_wrong: So it might be the immune thing after all. Even without the positive tests.

options.text: Psoriatic arthritis — DIP involvement is characteristic
options.feedback_wrong: DIP involvement does occur in psoriatic arthritis, but he has no rash, no nail changes, no family history of psoriasis, and the X-ray shows degenerative change not the pencil-in-cup deformity of psoriatic arthritis. The clinical picture doesn't support it.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A skin disease. I don't have any skin problems — just the hands.

options.text: Gout — uric acid should be checked urgently
options.feedback_wrong: Gout causes acute flares rather than gradual progressive bilateral hand pain. Uric acid was normal at 5.8. The X-ray shows no tophi, no joint erosions with overhanging edges. This is not gout.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Gout. My father had gout in his big toe. This has been both hands for a year.

management.title: First-line management
management.hint: Osteoarthritis of the hands — confirmed. What is the appropriate first-line approach for a 61-year-old with moderate symptoms and an occupation-specific functional goal?

options.text: Topical NSAIDs, thumb CMC splints, activity modification, occupational therapy referral
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Topical diclofenac directly to the affected joints — avoids systemic NSAID exposure. Splinting the first CMC joint reduces pain at the wheel. OT for adaptive techniques preserves the craft. This is the right approach for a stable, active man whose goal is to keep working.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "Topical first — the joint is accessible. Diclofenac gel directly where it hurts, systemic exposure avoided. The splint protects the CMC at the wheel. OT teaches him how to keep working around it."
options.pt_remark_correct: Something on the joint itself. A splint for the thumb. Someone to help me adapt the way I work. I can do all of that. I just want to keep throwing.

options.text: Methotrexate — DMARD therapy for joint protection
options.feedback_wrong: Methotrexate is for inflammatory arthritis — RA, psoriatic arthritis. It has no role in osteoarthritis, which is not an autoimmune condition. Prescribing it here exposes the patient to significant toxicity with no therapeutic benefit.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A strong immunosuppressant. For wear on the joints. Those are the same treatment?
options.fennick_endgame_comment: "Methotrexate for osteoarthritis — an immunosuppressant for a mechanical problem. Hepatotoxicity, bone marrow suppression, pulmonary toxicity. No benefit. Significant harm."
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "DMARDs are for inflammatory arthritis. This is osteoarthritis. Wrong mechanism."

options.text: Oral prednisolone course — reduce inflammation
options.feedback_wrong: Systemic steroids have no established role in hand OA. Long-term use causes significant harm — osteoporosis, hyperglycaemia, adrenal suppression. The same trap the Politician fell into, prescribed for the wrong thing.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Steroids. Long term. For something you just told me is mechanical wear.

options.text: Refer for surgical joint replacement
options.feedback_wrong: Surgery — CMC arthroplasty or fusion — is reserved for severe refractory OA that has failed all conservative measures. This patient has not tried conservative management yet. Surgery is not the first step.
options.pt_remark_wrong: An operation. On both hands. Before we've tried anything else.

management.title: Disposition and follow-up
management.hint: Stable, working, motivated to preserve his craft. What does the right discharge plan look like?

options.text: Discharge — topical NSAID, CMC splint, OT referral, GP follow-up in 6–8 weeks
options.feedback_correct: Exactly right. This is an outpatient condition. He goes home with a treatment plan that addresses the pain, protects the joint, and preserves the craft. OT is not optional — adaptive technique at the wheel is the difference between continuing and stopping.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "OT is the most important referral on this plan. For a man whose entire identity is in his hands, adaptive technique at the wheel is the difference between continuing and stopping."
options.pt_remark_correct: Home. With something to try. And someone who can help me figure out how to keep working around it. I picked up the rib this morning — just to see. It still knows where to go.

options.text: Admit for rheumatology inpatient review
options.feedback_wrong: There is no indication for admission. This is stable chronic OA. Inpatient rheumatology review serves no purpose here.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Admitted. For a year of gradual hand pain in a stable man.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Osteoarthritis does not require inpatient admission."

options.text: Discharge with oral ibuprofen only — review if worsens
options.feedback_wrong: Oral NSAIDs alone misses the opportunity for targeted topical treatment, joint protection with splinting, and OT. And in a 61-year-old with no current GI or renal issues, systemic NSAIDs carry risk that topical formulations avoid.
options.pt_remark_wrong: The same tablet I've been taking when it gets bad. And come back if it gets worse.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "NSAIDs alone without safety netting or physio is incomplete management."

options.text: Refer to orthopaedics for surgical planning
options.feedback_wrong: No conservative management has been tried. Orthopaedic referral for surgical planning at this stage bypasses everything that should come first.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A surgeon. Before we've tried the splint or the therapy or anything else.
options.fennick_endgame_comment: "Surgery before conservative management was tried. Arthroplasty carries real risk. He had not yet tried a splint."
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Surgery is the last step, not the first. Conservative management has not been tried."

report_template.presenting: one year of progressive bilateral hand pain worst at the thumb bases and DIP joints, worsening with use and easing with rest, with functional decline at the pottery wheel

pertinent_positives.addl-hx: pain worse with use and better with rest, morning stiffness under 20 minutes, DIP and first CMC joint involvement bilaterally, bony thickening, forty years of repetitive craft work
pertinent_positives.pe: Heberden's nodes at DIP joints, tender first CMC joints bilaterally, crepitus with thumb opposition, no synovitis, no ulnar deviation, no nodules
pertinent_positives.labs: RF negative, anti-CCP negative, ESR mildly elevated at 22 — nonspecific and age-appropriate, CRP borderline normal
pertinent_positives.imaging: hand X-rays showing osteophytes and joint space narrowing at DIP and first CMC joints bilaterally, no erosions, no periarticular osteopenia
pertinent_positives.special: negative RF and anti-CCP with nonspecific mild ESR elevation — inflammatory arthritis not supported

report_template.assessment: Osteoarthritis of the hands — bilateral, DIP and first CMC distribution, occupational pattern
report_template.plan: topical diclofenac to affected joints, first CMC splints, activity modification, occupational therapy referral for adaptive techniques, GP follow-up in 6-8 weeks, analgesia review

explanation: Osteoarthritis is the most common joint disease — mechanical degeneration of articular cartilage from wear, age, and loading. Hand OA classically involves DIP joints (Heberden's nodes), PIP joints (Bouchard's nodes), and the first CMC joint. Pain worse with use and better with rest distinguishes it from inflammatory arthritis. X-ray: osteophytes, joint space narrowing, subchondral sclerosis — no erosions. Management: topical NSAIDs, splinting, OT, analgesia. DMARDs and steroids have no role.

explanation_detail.who_gets_it: Adults over 50, more common in women. Risk factors: age, female sex, obesity, prior joint injury, repetitive occupation. Strong occupational association.
explanation_detail.risk_factors: Age, female sex, obesity, prior injury, repetitive mechanical loading, family history.
explanation_detail.typical_presentation: Gradual onset joint pain worse with use, better with rest. Morning stiffness under 30 minutes. Bony enlargement rather than soft tissue swelling. Heberden's nodes (DIP), Bouchard's nodes (PIP), first CMC tenderness. No systemic features.
explanation_detail.moa: Cartilage breakdown from mechanical stress exceeding repair capacity. Subchondral bone remodels, osteophytes form at joint margins. Synovial inflammation is secondary and mild — not the primary driver. Not autoimmune.
explanation_detail.step1_buzzwords: Heberden's nodes DIP, Bouchard's nodes PIP, first CMC, pain worse with use, osteophytes, joint space narrowing, no erosions, no synovitis, RF negative.
explanation_detail.lab_buzzwords: RF and anti-CCP negative. ESR/CRP normal or mildly elevated — nonspecific. X-ray: osteophytes, subchondral sclerosis, joint space narrowing, NO erosions. No periarticular osteopenia.
explanation_detail.treatment: Topical NSAIDs (diclofenac gel) first-line. Oral NSAIDs if topical insufficient. Splinting first CMC. Occupational therapy. Intra-articular steroid for flares. Surgery (CMC arthroplasty) only for severe refractory disease after conservative failure.

cc_after_addl_hx: The wheel still knows what to do. It's just the hands arguing with me now.
cc_after_pe: You're feeling the joints one by one. I could tell you which ones before you get to them.
cc_after_imaging: Wear. You're going to tell me it's wear. I think I knew that.
cc_after_hint: The morning stiffness. I did say it was gone by the time I'd walked to the studio.
cc_after_dx: So it's not that my hands forgot. They've just been wearing down. Forty years of the same motion — I suppose they were always going to say something eventually.
generic_lab_remark: Whatever helps you understand it. I'd like to know what's happening too.

title: The Stoker's PR

personality.voice: Completely sincere. Every concern is about performance and recovery. Never trying to be funny — just operating on different priorities than everyone else in the room.
personality.crack: When told he lost muscle. That's the only thing that lands.

patient.name: The Stoker
patient.occupation: Steam Engine Stoker, Ferrovane Central Rail

chart.cc: I hit a PR yesterday and this morning my urine is a strange colour. I think they might be related. I wanted to confirm.
chart.hpi: The Stoker arrived in work clothes, slightly stiff in the legs but otherwise appearing entirely well. He shovels coal for ten-hour shifts at the Ferrovane Central Rail depot — physical work, daily. Yesterday after his shift he set out to complete a personal goal he has been working toward for some time. He achieved it. This morning he noticed his urine was a dark brown colour, which he found interesting rather than alarming. He has bilateral thigh and lower back pain which he rates as acceptable. He is not sure the pain and the urine are connected but thought it worth mentioning together. He has had no fever, no burning on urination, no prior kidney problems.
chart.meds: Protein supplement daily. Creatine monohydrate. No prescribed medications.
chart.sh: Steam engine stoker, Ferrovane Central Rail. Lives alone. Non-smoker. Does not drink — interferes with recovery.

additional_hx: He is precise about the numbers. One thousand squats. One hour. No rest periods — rest periods are for the return journey, not the work. He drank approximately half a cup of water during. Leg sensation disappeared around repetition seven hundred, which he interpreted as a positive sign — he had heard of the zone and believed he had entered it. The last three hundred he describes as automatic. He finished, walked home, ate a large meal, slept well. He is proud of this. He would like it noted.
patient_quote_addl_hx: "One thousand. One hour. I have been working toward this for four months. The legs stopped feeling anything around seven hundred but I have read that is normal. That is the zone."
pe_findings: Well-built, muscular. Bilateral quadriceps and hamstring tenderness on palpation — significant, though patient rates pain as acceptable and attributes it to successful training. Mild lower back paraspinal tenderness. No joint swelling. Urine dipstick at bedside: 3+ blood with no red cells on microscopy — consistent with myoglobinuria. HR 96, BP 134/82. Mild pitting oedema bilateral ankles.
imaging: Renal ultrasound: bilateral kidneys normal size and echogenicity. No obstruction, no hydronephrosis, no stones. The kidneys appear structurally intact — the threat here is biochemical, not anatomical.

special_test.name: Creatine Kinase (CK) Level
special_test.result: CK 48,200 U/L. Creatinine 2.1 mg/dL. BUN 34. Potassium 5.8 mEq/L. LDH elevated. AST 680 U/L. ALT mildly elevated. Urine myoglobin positive.

memory_fragments.pe: Seven hundred repetitions in, his legs went quiet. He kept going. He thought that was the point.

tests.name: CBC
tests.pt_remark: Is that the one that shows red blood cells. I've read that endurance athletes have higher counts.

tests.name: CMP
tests.result: Creatinine 2.1 (elevated). BUN 34. K 5.8 (elevated). Ca 7.2 (low). Glucose normal. AST 680 (elevated). ALT mildly elevated. LFTs otherwise normal.
tests.pt_remark: I had a large meal after. Rice, chicken, two eggs. For recovery.

tests.name: EKG
tests.result: Sinus tachycardia rate 96. Peaked T waves in precordial leads. No PR prolongation. No sine wave pattern.
tests.pt_remark: Is my heart big. I've read that trained hearts are larger. Is that what this shows.

tests.name: Troponin
tests.result: Troponin I 0.06. Serial monitoring recommended.

tests.name: Lipase
tests.result: Normal. Not relevant.

tests.name: UA
tests.result: Dipstick: 3+ blood. Microscopy: no red blood cells. Urine dark brown in colour. Urine myoglobin assay positive.
tests.memory_fragment: Oh yeah — the dark urine. I thought that was normal. Like engine oil. When the machine is working hard.

tests.name: ABG/VBG
tests.result: pH 7.32. Bicarbonate 18. Lactate 3.8. pO2 96 on room air.

tests.name: Coags
tests.result: PT/INR 1.2 (mildly elevated). PTT 36. Monitor — severe rhabdomyolysis can rarely precipitate DIC.

hints: Cola-coloured urine with 3+ blood on dipstick but no red cells on microscopy. That is not haematuria. Something else is cross-reacting with the haem test. What does muscle breakdown release that looks like blood on dipstick?

CK 48,200. That number is not a lab error. Normal is under 200. His quadriceps ran out of fuel somewhere around repetition seven hundred and kept going anyway. What happens to muscle that contracts without oxygen?

Creatinine 2.1, potassium 5.8, peaked T waves on EKG. The kidneys are being hit from two directions — myoglobin precipitating in the tubules, and volume depletion from not drinking water for an hour of maximum exertion. The treatment is aggressive hydration to flush the tubes before they block completely.

The goal is to get urine output above 200-300mL per hour and keep urine pH alkaline — alkalinisation reduces myoglobin precipitation. The hyperkalaemia needs monitoring because it can reach fatal levels as more myocytes continue to break down.

management.title: Immediate management
management.hint: CK 48,200. AKI. Hyperkalaemia with EKG changes. What do you do first?

options.text: Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation — normal saline or Hartmann's
options.feedback_correct: Correct. The goal is urine output 200-300mL/hour. Myoglobin precipitates in acidic concentrated urine — dilute it and flush it before the tubules block completely. This is the single most important intervention.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "The kidney is not broken yet. It is being poisoned by what is arriving in the tubule. Flood it out before it blocks."
options.pt_remark_correct: I can still have protein with this, right doc. I have a shake in my bag.

options.text: Catheterise and monitor urine output only — await CK trend
options.feedback_wrong: Watching without treating while CK is 48,200 and creatinine is rising wastes the window to prevent complete renal tubular obstruction. Aggressive hydration must start now.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Wait and see. How long are we waiting. I have a shift tomorrow.

options.text: Treat as UTI — start antibiotics
options.feedback_wrong: There is no infection here. The dipstick blood is myoglobin, not bacteria. No dysuria, no fever, no cells on microscopy. Antibiotics treat nothing in this patient and waste time.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A urine infection. From squats.

options.text: Urgent dialysis referral
options.feedback_wrong: Dialysis is a last resort for refractory AKI that does not respond to aggressive hydration. His kidneys are not there yet — creatinine is 2.1, not anuric failure. Aggressive IV fluids are the first intervention. Dialysis may never be needed if you act now.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Dialysis. Is that like a machine that does what the kidneys do. Can it also build muscle.
options.fennick_endgame_comment: "Creatinine 2.1 with a rising trend is not dialysis territory yet. You bypassed the intervention that could have prevented it entirely."
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "CK 48200 without renal failure. IV fluids first — dialysis is not indicated yet."

management.title: Urine alkalinisation
management.hint: Myoglobin precipitates in acidic urine. How do you reduce that precipitation while the fluids are running?

options.text: Add sodium bicarbonate to IV fluids — target urine pH >6.5
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Alkaline urine reduces myoglobin precipitation in the tubules. Add sodium bicarbonate to the IV fluid regimen and monitor urine pH. Target above 6.5.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "Myoglobin is less soluble in acid. You are changing the chemistry of what arrives at the tubule, not just the volume."
options.pt_remark_correct: So the goal is to make the urine less acidic. Like adjusting the pH of a training environment.

options.text: Give furosemide to force diuresis
options.feedback_wrong: Furosemide in rhabdomyolysis is not first-line and can worsen volume depletion. The diuresis you want comes from aggressive fluid loading, not from forcing the kidney to excrete what it already can't handle.
options.pt_remark_wrong: More output. Yeah I want maximum output too.

options.text: Nothing further — IV fluids alone are sufficient
options.feedback_wrong: Fluids alone are the foundation but alkalinisation significantly reduces tubular precipitation of myoglobin. Adding bicarbonate is standard of care in severe rhabdomyolysis.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Just the drip. Keep it simple.

options.text: Vitamin C infusion — antioxidant protection
options.feedback_wrong: No evidence supports routine vitamin C infusion in rhabdomyolysis. The mechanism of tubular injury is physical precipitation, not oxidative damage. Fluids and alkalinisation are the proven interventions.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Vitamin C. For recovery. Yeah I actually take that already.

management.title: Manage the hyperkalaemia
management.hint: K 5.8 with peaked T waves. Creatinine rising. More myocytes are still breaking down. What do you do?

options.text: Cardiac monitoring, calcium gluconate if K rises further, salbutamol nebuliser, consider kayexalate
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Peaked T waves at K 5.8 means you are watching an EKG that could deteriorate. Calcium gluconate stabilises the myocardium if K rises. Salbutamol drives K into cells temporarily. Aggressive hydration itself will help as renal excretion improves.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "Every myocyte that continues to break down is releasing more potassium. The number will keep rising until the process stops. Stay ahead of it."
options.pt_remark_correct: So the real question is — when can I get back to the squats.

options.text: Reassure — K 5.8 is only mildly elevated
options.feedback_wrong: K 5.8 with EKG changes in a patient whose CK is 48,200 and still rising is not reassurance territory. This number will keep climbing as muscle breakdown continues. Peaked T waves are the warning before it becomes fatal.
options.pt_remark_wrong: It's fine. I feel fine.
options.fennick_endgame_comment: "K 5.8 with peaked T waves and ongoing muscle breakdown. You reassured him. The potassium continued to rise."
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Potassium 5.8 with a CK of 48200 and oliguria. This is not mild."

options.text: Immediate haemodialysis for hyperkalaemia
options.feedback_wrong: Dialysis is not first-line for K 5.8. Medical management — calcium, salbutamol, hydration — should be attempted first. Dialysis is reserved for refractory hyperkalaemia that does not respond to these measures.
options.pt_remark_wrong: The machine again. Is the machine also going to tell me when I can squat.

options.text: Oral potassium binders only
options.feedback_wrong: Oral binders act slowly over hours. With EKG changes and ongoing muscle breakdown, you need faster-acting interventions — IV calcium gluconate and salbutamol — while hydration improves renal excretion.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A tablet. For the heart thing. Yeah sure.

management.title: Disposition
management.hint: CK 48,200, AKI, hyperkalaemia with EKG changes. Where does he go?

options.text: Admit — aggressive IV fluids, telemetry, serial CK, creatinine, and potassium every 4-6 hours
options.feedback_correct: Correct. CK 48,200 with AKI and hyperkalaemia requires inpatient admission for aggressive hydration and close monitoring. CK will continue to rise for 24-48 hours before peaking. He needs telemetry for the hyperkalaemia and serial renal function.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "CK peaks 24 to 48 hours after the insult. He is not at the worst of it yet. He needs to be here when it arrives."
options.pt_remark_correct: How long am I in here. Also — I am probably the biggest guy in this hospital. Do you have a gym. Asking for recovery purposes.

options.text: Discharge with oral hydration and GP follow-up in one week
options.feedback_wrong: Absolutely not. CK 48,200, creatinine rising, potassium 5.8 with EKG changes. He cannot drink enough water at home to achieve the urine output required. He needs IV fluids at a rate that oral intake cannot match.
options.pt_remark_wrong: I can drink more at home. I have a two litre bottle.
options.fennick_endgame_comment: "CK still rising. Kidneys still failing. Potassium climbing toward an arrhythmia. You sent him home with a water bottle."
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "He needs IV fluids at a rate that cannot be matched orally."

options.text: Discharge once urine clears — visual inspection sufficient
options.feedback_wrong: Urine colour normalising does not mean CK has normalised. CK peaks 24-48 hours after the insult and the renal injury continues beyond when the urine clears. Visual inspection is not a discharge criterion.
options.pt_remark_wrong: The colour's already getting better I think. I checked this morning.
options.fennick_endgame_comment: "The urine cleared. The CK continued to rise for another 36 hours. The kidneys did not know the urine looked better."
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Visual inspection is not a substitute for urine myoglobin quantification."

options.text: ICU admission for immediate dialysis
options.feedback_wrong: ICU and dialysis are not yet indicated. He is not anuric, not haemodynamically unstable, and has not failed medical management. Monitored ward bed with aggressive IV fluids is the appropriate level of care right now.
options.pt_remark_wrong: ICU. That sounds serious. Can I still have my shake in there.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "No renal failure yet. Aggressive IV hydration is the treatment — not immediate dialysis."

report_template.presenting: cola-coloured urine and bilateral leg pain following one thousand squats in one hour

pertinent_positives.addl-hx: one thousand squats completed in one hour, minimal water intake during, loss of leg sensation around repetition seven hundred interpreted as positive sign
pertinent_positives.pe: significant bilateral quadriceps and hamstring tenderness, myoglobinuria on dipstick with no red cells on microscopy, mild ankle oedema
pertinent_positives.labs: CK 48,200 critically elevated, creatinine 2.1 AKI, potassium 5.8 hyperkalaemia, metabolic acidosis, AST elevated from muscle origin
pertinent_positives.imaging: renal ultrasound normal structure bilaterally — biochemical injury not anatomical
pertinent_positives.special: CK 48,200 confirming severe rhabdomyolysis with myoglobinuria and acute kidney injury

report_template.assessment: Rhabdomyolysis — exertional, severe, complicated by AKI and hyperkalaemia
report_template.plan: aggressive IV fluid resuscitation targeting urine output 200-300mL/hr, sodium bicarbonate alkalinisation, cardiac monitoring for hyperkalaemia, serial CK and renal function every 4-6 hours, admit to monitored ward

explanation: Rhabdomyolysis is the breakdown of skeletal muscle releasing myoglobin, CK, potassium, and other intracellular contents into the bloodstream. Myoglobin precipitates in renal tubules causing AKI. Classic triad: muscle pain, weakness, dark urine. Key: dipstick blood with no RBCs = myoglobinuria not haematuria. CK >1000 is diagnostic; >10,000 carries high AKI risk. Treatment: aggressive IV hydration to achieve urine output 200-300mL/hr. Alkalinise urine. Monitor potassium — hyperkalaemia can be fatal.

explanation_detail.who_gets_it: Exertional: extreme unaccustomed exercise, crush injury, seizures. Non-exertional: statin myopathy, viral myositis, metabolic myopathies, heatstroke, alcohol, cocaine.
explanation_detail.risk_factors: Unaccustomed extreme exercise, volume depletion during exertion, heat, stimulants, statins, genetic muscle enzyme deficiencies.
explanation_detail.typical_presentation: Muscle pain and weakness, dark cola-coloured urine, elevated CK. May present with AKI (creatinine rise), hyperkalaemia (peaked T waves, arrhythmia risk), hypocalcaemia (calcium deposits in necrotic muscle), elevated AST (muscle source).
explanation_detail.moa: Muscle cell membrane disruption → myoglobin, CK, K, Ca, phosphate release → myoglobin filtered by kidney → precipitates in acidic tubules → tubular obstruction and direct toxicity → AKI. Hyperkalaemia from intracellular K release can cause fatal arrhythmia.
explanation_detail.step1_buzzwords: Cola urine, dipstick blood no RBCs, CK markedly elevated, myoglobinuria, AKI, hyperkalaemia, aggressive IV fluids, alkalinise urine.
explanation_detail.lab_buzzwords: CK >1000 diagnostic, >10,000 high AKI risk. UA: dipstick 3+ blood, microscopy no RBCs. CMP: elevated creatinine, K, low Ca, high phosphate, elevated AST (muscle). EKG: peaked T waves from hyperkalaemia.
explanation_detail.treatment: Aggressive IV fluids — normal saline or Hartmann's, target urine output 200-300mL/hr. Sodium bicarbonate alkalinisation — target urine pH >6.5. Monitor K closely — calcium gluconate if EKG changes, salbutamol, consider kayexalate. Dialysis only if refractory AKI or hyperkalaemia.

cc_after_addl_hx: One thousand. One hour. The legs went quiet around seven hundred. I thought that was the zone.
cc_after_pe: More pain more gain, right doc.
cc_after_imaging: Are my kidneys big? I wish I could train them. Imagine kidney day.
cc_after_hint: So the urine thing is connected to the squats. I thought it might be.
cc_after_dx: So I lost muscle. ... How much. Specifically. That is important information.
fennick_after_pe: "Dipstick 3+ blood. No red cells on microscopy."
generic_lab_remark: Is there one that shows muscle damage specifically. Just curious.

title: The Madame's Breath

personality.voice: Acknowledges everything, takes nothing seriously. Discomfort is a mild inconvenience. Symptoms are someone else's problem to interpret. Makes her breathlessness sound like a rhetorical pause.
personality.crack: When she hears the severity. Just — 'I see.' That is all.

chart.cc: I am... fine. ... This is what not fine feels like, I assume.
chart.hpi: The Madame arrived alone, unhurried, in clothes that cost more than the ward's monthly coal budget. She has had progressive exertional breathlessness for approximately two years — initially only on the stairs, now on moderate exertion. She has no cough, no fever, no chest pain. She has had two episodes of near-fainting in the past six months, attributed at the time to not eating enough. She has noted her ankles swell by evening. She delayed coming because she was busy and assumed it would pass. She answers questions with perfect composure and occasional detachment, as if describing someone else's body.
chart.pmh: No significant medical history. No prior cardiac or respiratory diagnoses.
chart.fh: Mother had heart problems in her sixties. Details unknown.
chart.sh: Irongate district. Married, no children. Non-smoker. Does not work. Travels occasionally by carriage.

memory_fragments.pe: She noticed the stairs last winter. Not the breathlessness — the pause at the top. She had never paused before. She started leaving earlier so no one would see.
memory_fragments.imaging: The Irongate house has four floors. She has been sleeping on the second for eight months. She told her husband the upper rooms were too cold.

additional_hx: She is precise when asked directly. The breathlessness started on exertion — specifically the stairs at home. Four flights. She now stops at the second floor. She attributes this to being less active than she used to be. The near-fainting episodes occurred after standing quickly from a chair and after walking briskly to a carriage. Both resolved in minutes. The ankle swelling is bilateral, worse by evening, gone by morning. She has not told her husband how limited she has become. She mentions this as an afterthought, with no particular emotion.
patient_quote_addl_hx: "The stairs became... a project. I thought I was simply getting older. One does, I'm told."
pe_findings: Well-dressed, calm, mildly dyspnoeic at rest — she does not appear to notice. JVP elevated at 5cm above sternal angle. Loud P2 on auscultation. Right ventricular heave palpable at left sternal border. Mild pitting oedema bilateral ankles. No crackles, no wheeze. No clubbing. Peripherally cool.
imaging: CXR: enlarged main pulmonary artery. Peripheral pruning of pulmonary vessels. Mild cardiomegaly — right-sided. No pulmonary oedema. No consolidation. No pleural effusion.

special_test.name: Echocardiogram
special_test.result: RVSP estimated 74 mmHg. Right ventricular dilation and hypertrophy. Paradoxical septal motion. Tricuspid regurgitation moderate. Left ventricular function normal. Left heart structurally normal. Pericardial effusion trace.

tests.name: CBC
tests.result: WBC 7.2. Hgb 13.8. Plt 312. Mild erythrocytosis — Hct 42%.
tests.pt_remark: I assume this is standard. My private physician runs these quarterly.

tests.name: CMP
tests.result: Sodium 138. Creatinine 1.1. BUN 18. LFTs: AST 44, ALT 38, bilirubin 1.4 (mildly elevated). Uric acid 7.2 (elevated).
tests.pt_remark: I had wine at dinner last night. Two glasses. A good one. I assume that is not relevant.

tests.name: EKG
tests.result: Sinus tachycardia rate 96. Right axis deviation. Right bundle branch block. RV strain pattern — T wave inversions V1-V4. P pulmonale.

tests.name: Troponin
tests.result: Troponin I 0.04. Mildly elevated. Serial recommended.

tests.name: NT-proBNP
tests.result: NT-proBNP 1,840 pg/mL (elevated).
tests.pt_remark: That one has a very long name. What is it measuring.

tests.name: UA
tests.result: Normal. No protein. No blood. No infection.

tests.name: ABG/VBG
tests.result: pH 7.46. pO2 62 on room air. pCO2 32. HCO3 22. O2 sat 93%.

tests.name: Coags
tests.result: PT/INR 1.1. PTT 30. Normal.

hints: O2 93% at rest. JVP elevated. Loud P2. RV heave. No crackles, no wheeze. The lungs are clear — the problem is upstream of them.
Right axis deviation, RBBB, T wave inversions V1-V4. The right heart is under sustained pressure. Something is making it work harder than it should.
RVSP 74 mmHg on echo. Normal is under 25. The pulmonary vasculature is severely obstructed. This has been building for years. The left heart is entirely normal.

Progressive exertional dyspnoea, syncope, right heart strain on EKG and echo, elevated RVSP, clear lungs, no left heart disease. Two years of symptoms. The pulmonary arterial pressure is critically elevated.

management.title: Initial workup
management.hint: O2 93% at rest. JVP elevated. Loud P2. Clear lungs. Right heart strain on EKG. Where do you start?

options.text: Echocardiogram, ECG, NT-proBNP, full bloods
options.feedback_correct: Correct. The presentation points to right heart pathology — elevated JVP, loud P2, RV heave, clear lungs. Echo is the critical first step. NT-proBNP quantifies right heart stress. ECG has already shown right axis deviation and RV strain.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "Clear lungs, right heart strain. You are not looking at a lung problem. You are looking at what is happening to the right heart."
options.pt_remark_correct: A heart scan. ... I had the house surveyed last month for structural problems. ... Perhaps I should have surveyed the heart instead.

options.text: Spirometry and peak flow — assess for asthma or COPD
options.feedback_wrong: Spirometry will be near normal in PAH — the airways are not the problem. The clinical findings point to the right heart, not obstructive or restrictive lung disease. Asthma and COPD do not cause elevated JVP, loud P2, or RV heave.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Blow into a tube. ... My husband has a very good wind instrument specialist. ... I assume that is not relevant.

options.text: CT pulmonary angiogram — rule out pulmonary embolism
options.feedback_wrong: PE is reasonable in the differential but the chronic two-year progression, RV hypertrophy on examination, and absence of acute onset argue against acute PE. Echo and ECG first to characterise the right heart before committing to radiation and contrast.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A scan. How long does that take. ... I have a dinner. ... I will have the dinner moved.

options.text: Reassure — likely anxiety and deconditioning, discharge with lifestyle advice
options.feedback_wrong: O2 93% at rest. Elevated JVP. RV heave. Loud P2. This is not anxiety. Discharging this patient risks decompensation within days.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Anxiety. ... Yes. ... My decorator said the same thing about the wallpaper. ... He was also wrong.
options.fennick_endgame_comment: "O2 93% at rest. Right heart under critical pressure. You sent her home with lifestyle advice. She would not have made it to the end of the week."
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Right heart strain on echo, RVSP 68, severe functional limitation. This is not anxiety."

management.title: Interpret the echo — next step
management.hint: RVSP 74 mmHg. RV dilation and hypertrophy. Normal left heart. What does this tell you and what needs to happen next?

options.text: Right heart catheterisation — gold standard confirmation of PAH
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Echo strongly suggests pulmonary hypertension but right heart catheterisation is required for definitive diagnosis and classification — mean pulmonary arterial pressure, pulmonary vascular resistance, wedge pressure. You cannot start PAH-specific therapy without it.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "Echo suggests. Catheter confirms. You cannot start the right treatment without the right number."
options.pt_remark_correct: A catheter into the heart. ... Is that strictly necessary. ... Yes. ... I thought so. ... I will need a private room.

options.text: Start diuretics and discharge — right heart failure management
options.feedback_wrong: Diuretics address the congestion but not the underlying elevated pulmonary vascular resistance. Without PAH-specific therapy the right heart will continue to fail. And you have not yet confirmed the diagnosis or excluded secondary causes.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A water tablet. ... I have been to three spas this year. ... None of them helped either.

options.text: Start beta blocker — control heart rate
options.feedback_wrong: Beta blockers are contraindicated in PAH. The right ventricle is compensating for massively elevated pulmonary pressures through tachycardia and hypertrophy. Blocking that compensatory response can precipitate acute right heart failure.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Something for the heart rate. ... My jeweller recommended something similar once. ... That also did not work.
options.fennick_endgame_comment: "The right ventricle is surviving on tachycardia. You removed it. The RV had nothing left to compensate with."
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Beta-blockade worsens right heart failure in PAH. Contraindicated."

options.text: Repeat echo in three months — monitor progression
options.feedback_wrong: RVSP 74 mmHg with RV dilation and O2 93% at rest is not a watch-and-wait situation. This patient needs urgent specialist input and haemodynamic confirmation now.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Come back in three months. ... Yes. ... I will have my secretary arrange it.
options.fennick_endgame_comment: "RVSP 74. RV dilation. O2 93% at rest. You scheduled a follow-up appointment. She decompensated before it."
