options.fennick_wrong_comment: "She cannot walk to the bathroom without desaturating. Three months is not acceptable."

management.title: Exclude secondary causes
management.hint: PAH confirmed on catheter. Before starting therapy — what underlying causes need to be ruled out?

options.text: Connective tissue disease screen, HIV, LFTs, TFTs, V/Q scan for CTEPH
options.feedback_correct: Correct. PAH is frequently secondary — connective tissue disease, HIV, portal hypertension, thyroid disease, and chronic thromboembolic disease all cause pulmonary hypertension and require different management. V/Q scan specifically rules out CTEPH which is surgically treatable.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "CTEPH is operable. If you miss it you have treated the wrong disease with the wrong drugs."
options.pt_remark_correct: More tests. A lung scan. ... I had the air quality tested in every room last spring. ... Apparently that is not the same thing.

options.text: Skip secondary workup — start PAH therapy immediately
options.feedback_wrong: Starting PAH-specific therapy without ruling out secondary causes risks missing a treatable underlying condition — particularly CTEPH, which is potentially curable with surgery. Connective tissue disease changes the prognosis and monitoring requirements.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Skip the tests. Just treat it. ... I prefer that approach. ... My tailor works the same way.

options.text: Lung biopsy to confirm diagnosis
options.feedback_wrong: Lung biopsy is not part of PAH workup and carries serious risk in a patient with severely elevated pulmonary pressures. The diagnosis is haemodynamic, not histological.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A biopsy. Of the lung. ... While it is still in use.
options.fennick_endgame_comment: "Biopsy in severely elevated pulmonary pressures. The bleeding risk alone. This was not a histological diagnosis."
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Biopsy is not part of the PAH diagnostic algorithm. RHC is."

options.text: Refer to psychiatry — symptom exaggeration cannot be excluded
options.feedback_wrong: O2 93%, RVSP 74 mmHg, right heart failure on examination. There is nothing psychiatric about this presentation.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A psychiatrist. ... I am not sure whether to be offended or amused. ... Both, I think. ... I will send him a gift either way.
options.fennick_endgame_comment: "The right heart was in failure. You sent her to psychiatry."
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Her echo is objective. Her desaturation is objective. Psychiatry is not indicated."

management.title: First-line PAH therapy
management.hint: Idiopathic PAH confirmed. Secondary causes excluded. WHO functional class III. What do you start?

options.text: PDE-5 inhibitor (sildenafil) + endothelin receptor antagonist (ambrisentan) — combination therapy
options.feedback_correct: Correct. AMBITION trial demonstrated superiority of upfront combination therapy over monotherapy in treatment-naive PAH. Sildenafil reduces pulmonary vascular resistance via cGMP pathway. Ambrisentan blocks endothelin-1 vasoconstriction. WHO class III warrants aggressive initial therapy.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "Two pathways. Both obstructed. Two drugs. This is not overtreatment — this is the trial data."
options.pt_remark_correct: Two medications. Daily. ... I see. ... I will have a cabinet made for them.

options.text: Sildenafil monotherapy only
options.feedback_wrong: Monotherapy is no longer first-line for WHO class III PAH in treatment-naive patients. AMBITION trial showed combination therapy significantly reduces clinical worsening events. Class III disease warrants upfront combination.
options.pt_remark_wrong: One tablet. ... That seems more proportionate.

options.text: Long-term anticoagulation only
options.feedback_wrong: Anticoagulation may be considered as adjunct therapy in idiopathic PAH but it is not primary treatment. It does not address the elevated pulmonary vascular resistance. The vasculopathy requires PAH-specific agents.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A blood thinner. ... I had the carpets thinned last year as well. ... The house keeps requiring maintenance.

options.text: Calcium channel blocker — vasoreactivity testing positive
options.feedback_wrong: Calcium channel blockers are only appropriate in the small subset of PAH patients who demonstrate a positive vasoreactivity test on right heart catheterisation. Starting CCB empirically without confirmed vasoreactivity can be harmful.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Something my mother took. ... She died at sixty-one. ... I am not sure that's relevant. ... Probably worth mentioning.

management.title: Disposition
management.hint: Treatment initiated. WHO class III PAH, RVSP 74, RV failure. Where does she go?

options.text: Admit — cardiology, pulmonology, diuresis, oxygen, urgent specialist centre referral
options.feedback_correct: Correct. WHO class III PAH with right heart failure requires admission. Diuresis for the congestion, supplemental oxygen, cardiac monitoring, and urgent referral to a specialist PAH centre for ongoing management and transplant assessment if needed.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "She needs a centre that sees this every week, not every year."
options.pt_remark_correct: Admitted. ... I suppose I should tell my husband. ... He will worry. ... Good. He should. ... He also widened the stairs.

options.text: Discharge with prescriptions — outpatient PAH clinic in 6 weeks
options.feedback_wrong: O2 93% at rest, right heart failure, RVSP 74. She cannot wait 6 weeks. Outpatient initiation of PAH therapy is appropriate for stable class II patients — not class III with active right heart failure.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Six weeks. ... Yes. That suits my schedule. ... I have the stairs to deal with in the meantime.
options.fennick_endgame_comment: "Class III. Active right heart failure. O2 93% at rest. You sent her home with a prescription and a six-week appointment."
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "She needs same-day specialist review, not a six-week wait."

options.text: Discharge — reassure that medication will resolve symptoms within days
options.feedback_wrong: PAH therapy improves symptoms over weeks to months — it does not resolve them within days. And this patient cannot safely be discharged with active right heart failure.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Better in a few days. ... Excellent. ... I will tell my husband the stairs were the problem after all.
options.fennick_endgame_comment: "You told her she would feel better in a few days. She deteriorated overnight."
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "PAH medications take weeks to show effect. Discharging without monitoring is unsafe."

options.text: ICU admission — intubation and mechanical ventilation
options.feedback_wrong: Intubation in severe PAH can precipitate acute right heart failure and cardiovascular collapse — positive pressure ventilation dramatically increases pulmonary vascular resistance. Intubation is a last resort, not a first step in a conscious, communicating patient.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A tube in the throat. ... That does seem excessive. ... Though I did once commission a very aggressive renovation.
options.fennick_endgame_comment: "Positive pressure ventilation in severe PAH. The right ventricle had nothing left to push against the increased resistance."
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Intubation in PAH dramatically increases mortality. Avoid unless absolutely unavoidable."

report_template.presenting: two years of progressive exertional dyspnoea, two syncopal episodes, bilateral ankle oedema

pertinent_positives.addl-hx: progressive limitation from four flights to two floors over eight months, two near-syncopal episodes, bilateral ankle oedema worse by evening
pertinent_positives.pe: elevated JVP, loud P2, right ventricular heave, mild pitting oedema, O2 93% on room air, peripherally cool, clear lungs
pertinent_positives.labs: NT-proBNP 1840, mildly elevated troponin, right axis deviation and RV strain on EKG, mild erythrocytosis
pertinent_positives.imaging: CXR enlarged main pulmonary artery, peripheral pruning, right-sided cardiomegaly
pertinent_positives.special: RVSP 74 mmHg on echo, RV dilation and hypertrophy, normal left heart

report_template.assessment: Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension — idiopathic, WHO functional class III, right heart failure
report_template.plan: right heart catheterisation, secondary cause exclusion, upfront combination PDE-5i and ERA therapy, admit cardiology with specialist PAH centre referral

explanation: Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension is abnormally elevated pressure in the pulmonary arterial circulation (mPAP >20mmHg at rest on RHC) causing progressive RV failure. Idiopathic PAH affects women more than men, peak onset 20-50. Symptoms: progressive exertional dyspnoea, fatigue, syncope, right heart failure. Examination: loud P2, RV heave, elevated JVP, peripheral oedema. ECG: right axis deviation, RBBB, RV strain. Echo: elevated RVSP, RV dilation. Confirmed by right heart catheterisation. Treatment: PDE-5i + ERA combination, anticoagulation, diuretics, oxygen.

explanation_detail.who_gets_it: Women > men (2:1), age 20-50. Idiopathic, heritable (BMPR2 mutation), or associated with CTD, HIV, portal hypertension, congenital heart disease.
explanation_detail.risk_factors: Female sex, BMPR2 mutation, connective tissue disease (especially scleroderma), HIV, portal hypertension, anorexigen use.
explanation_detail.typical_presentation: Progressive exertional dyspnoea over months-years. Syncope on exertion. Right heart failure: elevated JVP, RV heave, loud P2, peripheral oedema. O2 reduced. Clear lungs.
explanation_detail.moa: Pulmonary vascular remodelling — endothelial dysfunction, smooth muscle proliferation, vasoconstriction. Elevated PVR → RV pressure overload → RV hypertrophy → RV failure → reduced cardiac output.
explanation_detail.step1_buzzwords: Loud P2, RV heave, right axis deviation, RBBB, RV strain V1-V4, RVSP >25mmHg, clear lungs, young-middle aged woman, exertional dyspnoea and syncope.
explanation_detail.lab_buzzwords: Elevated NT-proBNP, mildly elevated troponin (RV strain), ECG right axis + RBBB + T inversions V1-V4, Echo RVSP elevated + RV dilation, RHC gold standard mPAP >20mmHg + PAWP <15mmHg.
explanation_detail.treatment: WHO class I-II: monotherapy PDE-5i or ERA. WHO class III-IV: upfront combination therapy (AMBITION trial). Adjunct: diuretics, anticoagulation, oxygen. Advanced: IV prostacyclin (epoprostenol), lung transplant.

fennick_opening: "Two years. She came after two years."
cc_after_addl_hx: The stairs became... a project. ... I had them widened. ... It did not help.
cc_after_pe: You keep listening to my chest. ... I had the same concern. ... I bought a new mattress. ... Also did not help.
cc_after_imaging: The vessels look wrong. ... I thought it might be the ceiling. ... Should I buy a different house.
cc_after_hint: You look concerned. ... I find that more alarming than the breathing, frankly.
cc_after_dx: I see. ... How long has this been happening. ... I see. ... I should have bought a different house earlier.
fennick_after_addl_hx: "Two years of stairs. She told no one."
fennick_case_complete: "Two years. She adapted around it until she couldn't."
"She will need a specialist centre. Make sure she goes."
pt_opening: You look stressed. ... I have a physician for that. Shall I give you his name.
generic_lab_remark: My private physician runs these quarterly. I assume yours are comparable.

title: The Banker's Certainty

personality.voice: Completely convinced he is dying. Has done the reading. Has prepared documentation. Speaks in complete sentences with citations. Falls apart slightly when told he is fine.
personality.crack: "I don't? ... My documents say otherwise." and at the end: "So I really, really don't have cancer."

patient.name: The Banker
patient.occupation: Ledger Clerk, Ferrovane Bank of Commerce, Ledger Row

chart.cc: I have had a progressive bilateral headache for three weeks. (pulls out a document) It is consistent with my symptoms.
chart.hpi: The Banker arrived precisely on time, carrying a leather folder. He has had a bilateral pressing headache for three weeks, worst by end of shift, improved somewhat on rest days. No vomiting, no visual disturbance, no fever, no neck stiffness, no focal neurological symptoms. He works ten-hour days reviewing ledgers at the Ferrovane Bank of Commerce. He has not missed a day of work in six months until today. He took a half-day for personal reasons. He has brought documentation.
chart.pmh: No significant medical history. No prior headaches of note.
chart.meds: None. He considered taking something but wanted to establish the diagnosis first.
chart.fh: Father: hypertension. No family history of malignancy that he is aware of, though he notes this absence of evidence is not evidence of absence.
chart.sh: Ledger clerk, Ledger Row. Lives alone. Non-smoker. Occasional drink on Fridays — suspended since the headaches began, as a precaution.

additional_hx: On direct questioning he confirms the headache is bilateral, pressing or tightening in quality, mild to moderate intensity. It does not throb. It is not worsened by routine activity. No nausea, no photophobia, no phonophobia beyond mild sensitivity. Duration: four to six hours per episode, sometimes all day. Onset correlates precisely with extended desk work — he has cross-referenced this against his work schedule. He sleeps poorly and has increased his reading hours in the evenings since the headaches began, which he acknowledges may not have helped. He presents a handwritten table of symptom onset times, duration, and associated activities. It is remarkably well-organised.
patient_quote_addl_hx: "I brought evidence. (places a substantial stack of papers on the desk) The symptom onset pattern is on page two."
pe_findings: Alert, well-dressed, visibly anxious. Cranial nerves II-XII intact. No papilloedema on fundoscopy. No focal neurological deficit. Neck supple — no meningism. Bilateral pericranial muscle tenderness on palpation — frontalis, temporalis, trapezius. No scalp lesions. Pupils equal and reactive. Vision 6/6 bilaterally. No lymphadenopathy.
imaging: CT head without contrast: no intracranial mass, no haemorrhage, no midline shift, no hydrocephalus, no bony abnormality. Normal study.

special_test.name: Fundoscopy
special_test.result: No papilloedema. Disc margins sharp bilaterally. Vessels normal calibre. No haemorrhage, no exudates.

memory_fragments.pe: He noticed the headaches start exactly at the three-hour mark of ledger work. He wrote this down. He also wrote down that writing it down gave him a headache.

tests.name: CBC
tests.result: Normal. WBC 6.8, Hgb 15.2, Plt 298.
tests.pt_remark: I believe the relevant markers here would be haemoglobin and white cell count. Based on my reading.

tests.name: CMP
tests.result: Normal. Sodium 140, creatinine 0.9, glucose 88. LFTs normal.
tests.pt_remark: I fasted for twelve hours before coming. I read that was recommended. I also brought water. Was water acceptable.

tests.name: EKG
tests.result: Normal sinus rhythm. Not indicated for this presentation.
tests.pt_remark: I read that this one can detect abnormalities. Mine came back normal when I had it done two years ago. Is that still relevant.

tests.name: Troponin
tests.result: Negative. Not indicated.

tests.name: Lipase
tests.result: Normal. Not indicated.

tests.name: UA
tests.result: Normal. No abnormalities.

tests.name: ABG/VBG
tests.result: Normal. Not indicated.

tests.name: Coags
tests.result: Normal. Not indicated.

hints: Bilateral, pressing, non-pulsating headache. Not worsened by routine activity. No nausea, no photophobia, no aura. Onset after prolonged desk work, improved on rest days. Pericranial muscle tenderness on examination. The pattern is consistent and reproducible.
No papilloedema, no focal deficit, no meningism, no fever, no thunderclap onset. The neurological examination is entirely normal. Red flags are absent.
CT head is normal. No mass, no bleed, no raised intracranial pressure. The imaging is there to reassure — not because the presentation warrants it clinically.
Bilateral pressing headache, work-related onset, muscle tenderness, normal examination, normal imaging. The diagnosis is clinical. It does not require further workup.

management.title: Initial approach
management.hint: Normal examination, normal vitals. Bilateral pressing headache, work-triggered. What do you do first?

options.text: Clinical diagnosis — take full headache history and examine
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Tension headache is a clinical diagnosis. The history — bilateral, pressing, non-pulsating, work-related, no associated features — is characteristic. Examination confirms no red flags. No imaging required at this stage.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "Bilateral. Pressing. No aura. No nausea. Work-related. The diagnosis is in the history."
options.pt_remark_correct: You're just going to ask me questions? ... I have a document. ... It has graphs.

options.text: Urgent MRI brain — exclude intracranial mass
options.feedback_wrong: No red flags are present — no thunderclap onset, no papilloedema, no focal deficit, no fever, no progressive worsening over days. MRI is not indicated as the first step for a classic tension-type headache presentation.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Yes. The MRI. That's what I came for. ... Page four of my document recommends it.

options.text: Lumbar puncture — exclude subarachnoid haemorrhage
options.feedback_wrong: LP is indicated for thunderclap headache or suspected meningitis — not a three-week progressive bilateral pressing headache with a clear precipitant and normal examination. There is no clinical indication here.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A needle in the spine. ... That does seem proportionate to my situation.

options.text: Reassure and discharge without assessment
options.feedback_wrong: A three-week headache warrants proper assessment before discharge, even if the likely diagnosis is benign. History and examination should be completed first.
options.pt_remark_wrong: You haven't even looked at my document.

management.title: Red flag assessment
management.hint: Three weeks of headache. What red flags need to be excluded before diagnosing tension headache?

options.text: Exclude: thunderclap onset, progressive worsening, fever/meningism, focal deficit, papilloedema, age >50 new headache
options.feedback_correct: Correct. None of these are present. Onset was gradual over weeks, not thunderclap. No fever, no neck stiffness. Neurological exam normal. No papilloedema on fundoscopy. The headache improves on rest days. Red flags are absent.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "No red flags. The examination is normal. The pattern is benign. Move forward."
options.pt_remark_correct: So none of the serious things. ... That's what my document said too. ... Different conclusion though.

options.text: Order CT head immediately — three weeks is too long to ignore
options.feedback_wrong: Duration alone does not indicate CT. Three weeks of bilateral pressing headache with a clear work-related pattern and normal examination does not meet imaging criteria. Red flags drive imaging decisions, not anxiety.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Yes. The CT. I've been waiting for someone to say CT. ... I've been waiting three weeks.

options.text: Refer to neurology before making any diagnosis
options.feedback_wrong: Tension headache is a primary care diagnosis. Neurology referral is appropriate for atypical features, treatment-refractory cases, or diagnostic uncertainty — not a textbook presentation with no red flags.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A specialist. Yes. ... I had assumed this would require a team.

options.text: Check inflammatory markers — exclude temporal arteritis
options.feedback_wrong: Temporal arteritis presents in patients over 50 with new unilateral temporal headache, jaw claudication, and elevated ESR. This patient is 24 with bilateral pressing headache. Temporal arteritis is not in the differential.
options.pt_remark_wrong: I did consider vasculitis. ... It was on page seven.

management.title: Diagnosis
management.hint: Bilateral pressing headache, work-related, muscle tenderness, normal examination, no red flags. What is the diagnosis?

options.text: Tension-type headache — episodic, work-related
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Bilateral, pressing or tightening quality, mild to moderate intensity, not aggravated by routine activity, no nausea or vomiting, pericranial muscle tenderness. Episodic tension-type headache, likely triggered by prolonged visual concentration and poor posture.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "Pericranial muscle tension. Prolonged near work. Poor sleep. This is the diagnosis."
options.pt_remark_correct: I don't have a tumour? ... My documents say otherwise. ... Are you certain.

options.text: Migraine without aura
options.feedback_wrong: Migraine is unilateral, pulsating, moderate to severe, worsened by activity, with nausea or photo/phonophobia. This headache is bilateral, pressing, mild to moderate, not worsened by activity. The pattern does not fit migraine.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Migraine. ... I hadn't considered migraine. ... Does migraine have a high mortality rate.

options.text: Intracranial hypertension — refer for LP
options.feedback_wrong: Raised ICP causes progressive headache worse in the morning, worse lying down, with papilloedema. This patient has normal fundoscopy, headache that improves on rest days, and a clear work-related pattern. Raised ICP does not fit.
options.pt_remark_wrong: High pressure in the brain. ... That sounds about right actually.

options.text: Cluster headache
options.feedback_wrong: Cluster headache is strictly unilateral, excruciating, periorbital, with autonomic features — lacrimation, nasal congestion, ptosis. Episodes last 15-180 minutes. This presentation shares none of those features.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Cluster. ... Is that worse than what I thought I had.

management.title: Management
management.hint: Tension headache confirmed. What is appropriate first-line management?

options.text: Simple analgesia, posture advice, screen breaks, sleep hygiene, reassurance
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Simple analgesia — paracetamol or ibuprofen — for acute episodes. Identify and address triggers: prolonged near work, poor posture, sleep disruption. Regular screen breaks. Reassurance is a significant part of the treatment in this case.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "Paracetamol. Screen breaks. Sleep. And stop reading medical textbooks after hours."
options.pt_remark_correct: Paracetamol. ... I have been avoiding it in case it masked the symptoms. ... I see. ... So I have been having headaches about having headaches.

options.text: Start prophylactic topiramate
options.feedback_wrong: Topiramate prophylaxis is for frequent migraine or chronic daily headache. Episodic tension headache triggered by a clear occupational cause does not require prophylactic medication — it requires addressing the cause.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A daily medication. ... For how long. ... I'll need to log this.

options.text: Refer for neurology and MRI before any treatment
options.feedback_wrong: The diagnosis is clear, the examination is normal, red flags are absent. Deferring treatment while awaiting neurology and MRI is not appropriate for episodic tension headache with an identifiable trigger.
options.pt_remark_wrong: More waiting. ... I can wait. ... I brought more documents.

options.text: Prescribe triptans
options.feedback_wrong: Triptans are for migraine and cluster headache — not tension headache. They act on serotonin receptors to abort a migraine attack. They are not effective for tension headache and are not indicated here.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Something stronger. ... Yes. That seems more appropriate for my situation.

management.title: Disposition
management.hint: Tension headache, no red flags, triggers identified. Where does he go?

options.text: Discharge — analgesia, lifestyle advice, GP follow-up if no improvement in 4-6 weeks
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Episodic tension headache with identified triggers is managed outpatient. Safety net: return if headache character changes, new neurological symptoms, or no improvement with conservative measures.
options.pt_remark_correct: So I really, really don't have cancer. ... I wrote a will. ... It was quite good actually.

options.text: Admit for observation and serial neurological examinations
options.feedback_wrong: There is no indication for admission. Tension headache is an outpatient condition. Normal examination, no red flags, identified triggers, and a clear management plan make admission entirely unnecessary.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Admitted. ... For how long. ... I'll need to notify the bank.
options.fennick_endgame_comment: "Admitted for tension headache. You occupied a bed that someone else needed."
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Normal examination, no red flags, clear diagnosis. He occupied a bed that someone else needed."

options.text: Discharge with no advice or follow-up plan
options.feedback_wrong: Discharging without advice on triggers, analgesia guidance, and a safety net misses the point of the consultation. He needs to understand what caused the headaches and what to do if they change.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Just go. ... That's it. ... I spent three weeks on this.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "He leaves without knowing what caused it or what to do if it changes."

options.text: Emergency CT head before discharge
options.feedback_wrong: No red flags. Normal examination. Clear diagnosis. CT adds radiation exposure and no clinical value here. The diagnosis does not require imaging confirmation.
options.pt_remark_wrong: The CT. Finally. ... Can I keep the images.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "No red flags. Normal examination. The radiation adds nothing here."

report_template.presenting: three weeks of bilateral pressing headache, worst after prolonged desk work, improved on rest days

pertinent_positives.addl-hx: work-related onset, ten-hour shifts of ledger review, poor sleep, bilateral pressing quality, duration four to six hours, no associated features
pertinent_positives.pe: bilateral pericranial muscle tenderness, no papilloedema, no focal deficit, no meningism, normal cranial nerves
pertinent_positives.imaging: CT head normal — no mass, no haemorrhage, no raised intracranial pressure
pertinent_positives.special: fundoscopy normal — no papilloedema, sharp disc margins

explanation: Tension-type headache is the most common primary headache disorder. Bilateral, pressing or tightening quality, mild to moderate intensity, not aggravated by routine activity, no nausea/vomiting, no more than one of photophobia or phonophobia. Episodic: under 15 days per month. Common triggers: stress, poor posture, sleep disruption, prolonged near work. Management: simple analgesia, trigger identification, lifestyle modification. No imaging required in absence of red flags.

explanation_detail.who_gets_it: Most common headache disorder — affects up to 78% of the general population at some point. All ages, slight female predominance.
explanation_detail.risk_factors: Stress, poor posture, sleep disruption, dehydration, prolonged near work, anxiety, depression.
explanation_detail.typical_presentation: Bilateral pressing or tightening headache, mild to moderate. Not pulsating. Not worsened by routine activity. No nausea. Pericranial muscle tenderness. Duration 30 minutes to several hours.
explanation_detail.moa: Peripheral sensitisation of pericranial myofascial tissues. Central sensitisation in chronic cases. Serotonin, nitric oxide, and CGRP pathways implicated.
explanation_detail.step1_buzzwords: Bilateral pressing headache, not pulsating, not worsened by activity, no nausea, pericranial tenderness, stress/posture trigger, normal examination, no red flags.
explanation_detail.lab_buzzwords: All normal. Diagnosis is clinical. Imaging only if red flags present: thunderclap, progressive, fever, focal deficit, papilloedema, age >50 new headache.
explanation_detail.treatment: Acute: paracetamol, NSAIDs, aspirin. Prophylaxis for chronic TTH: amitriptyline (first line), mirtazapine, venlafaxine. Lifestyle: trigger avoidance, posture, sleep, stress management.
